title: Entrepreneurial Competence Compass

introduction.subtitle: Competence assessment inspired by EntreComp. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Entrepreneurial Competence Compass helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Opportunity & Vision, Resource Mobilisation, Action & Experimentation, Ethics & Shared Value. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: When you notice a recurring frustration, what do you do?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Opportunity & Vision; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Opportunity & Vision, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Opportunity & Vision has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Opportunity & Vision is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Opportunity & Vision: When you notice a recurring frustration, what do you do
conceptHelp.summary: Opportunity & Vision means turning a noticed problem into a credible opportunity, a useful future state, and a reason for others to care. This question narrows the concept to "When you notice a recurring frustration, what do you do?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Opportunity & Vision matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "When you notice a recurring frustration, what do you do?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

conceptHelp.title: Action & Experimentation: How do you act when important information is missing

questions.text: How do you decide which customer pain deserves focus first?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows selecting the first customer pain; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of selecting the first customer pain, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show selecting the first customer pain in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Selecting the first customer pain is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Opportunity & Vision: How do you decide which customer pain deserves focus first
conceptHelp.summary: Opportunity & Vision is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How do you decide which customer pain deserves focus first?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Opportunity & Vision matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
